article_title: DifyTap: Four Authorization Flaws Let Attackers Silently Wiretap AI Chats Across Tenants on a Platform Powering Over 1 Million Apps
summary: All four CVE IDs and CVSS scores verified against NVD snapshots, all quotes verbatim, version discrepancy handled; only non-blocking allowlist/403 warnings remain — no correction needed.

content_preview.title: DifyTap: Four Authorization Flaws Let Attackers Silently Wiretap AI Chats Across Tenants on a Platform Powering Over 1 Million Apps
content_preview.summary: Zafran Security disclosed four authorization flaws in the open-source LLMOps platform Dify, including two critical bugs that let an attacker redirect another tenant's AI conversations to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Security researchers at Zafran Security have disclosed four authorization vulnerabilities in Dify, the open-source LLMOps platform, that can let an attacker silently intercept the prompts and model responses flowing through applications built by other organizations. According to [Zafran Security](https://www.zafran.io/resources/difytap-zafran-discovers-how-attackers-can-silently-wiretap-ai-data-across-tenants-on-a-platform-powering-1m-apps), which named the cluster of flaws DifyTap,...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured Analysis piece (944 words, within the 800-2000 range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections, each CVE broken out under its own subhead. Technical depth is appropriate and the wiretap framing is grounded in the cited tracing-redirect mechanism rather than sensationalized.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all five captured snapshots (source-0 Zafran, source-1 SecurityWeek, source-2 Security Affairs, source-3 NVD CVE-2026-41947, source-4 NVD CVE-2026-41948). source-5 (cybersecuritynews.com) returned HTTP 403 with a null snapshot, so I verified its cited claims via live WebFetch. NVD source-3 confirms CVE-2026-41947 CVSS 3.x base 9.1 CRITICAL and the verbatim quotes ('allows authenticated editor users to set and enable trace configurations for any application regardless of tenant ownership', 'redirect all messages and responses from victim applications to attacker-controlled LLM trace providers', 'Dify Cloud allows unauthenticated free self-registration, making account creation trivially accessible to any attacker'). NVD source-4 confirms CVE-2026-41948 CVSS 3.x base 9.4 CRITICAL, the 'version 1.14.1 and prior' wording and the path-traversal/Plugin-Daemon quote. Zafran source-0 confirms the persistent-exfiltration-channel quote, the '3,000 characters' document-preview quote, 1.14.2 as the partially patched version (May 19), 1.15.0 as the fully patched version (Jun 25) covering all four CVEs, the WAF-for-1.14.2 recommendation targeting CVE-2026-41948, and responsible disclosure. Security Affairs source-2 confirms the four CVE IDs, 9.1/9.4 scores, 'over a million applications across over 60 industries', the exfiltration quote (attributed by SA to the advisory), and the file-access paraphrases. SecurityWeek source-1 confirms the DifyTap name and 'over 1 million applications'. Cyber Security News (live fetch) confirms 140,000 GitHub stars / 10M Docker pulls, the Volvo/Maersk/Panasonic/Thermo Fisher enterprise list, RAG pipelines, 1.14.2 fixing 41947/41949/41950, the 41948 fix 'merged and is expected in an upcoming release', the unauthenticated GET/POST plugin-daemon exploitation, and the cross-tenant document-preview/UUID file access.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Rule 9 applied. All four CVE IDs (41947, 41948, 41949, 41950) are real and match NVD; the two CVSS scores attached in the body (9.1 and 9.4) match the NVD CVSS 3.x base scores exactly — none are fabricated. CVE descriptions cited to NVD; the 'over 1 million apps' figure and cross-tenant trace-endpoint mechanism cited to Zafran/Security Affairs; patch specifics (1.14.2 partial, 1.15.0 full, WAF mitigation) traced to Cyber Security News and Zafran. Version discrepancy is handled correctly: the Patching section explicitly distinguishes 1.14.2 (partial, three CVEs per Cyber Security News) from 1.15.0 (all four CVEs per Zafran). The Hacker News and Dark Reading are not cited. No orphan URLs — every body-linked domain (zafran.io, securityweek.com, securityaffairs.com, nvd.nist.gov x2, cybersecuritynews.com) appears in article.sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. 'Wiretap' is used because it is the disclosure's own framing and is justified by the persistent-redirect mechanism. The What We Don't Know section appropriately flags the absence of in-the-wild exploitation and the cloud-vs-self-hosted exposure uncertainty.
editor_notes.originality: Original. No prior Dify or DifyTap coverage exists in the archive. The 'broader pattern The Machine Herald has tracked' line is a soft, non-specific editorial reference and does not fabricate a named prior article.
recommendations: zafran.io and cybersecuritynews.com are not in config/source_allowlist.txt (non-blocking). Both are legitimate for this story — Zafran is the primary disclosing researcher and Cyber Security News is the reporting outlet; a maintainer may consider adding them.
The cybersecuritynews.com snapshot failed with HTTP 403 (bot-blocked) so no archive copy exists; its claims were verified via live WebFetch instead. Non-blocking.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, accurate cybersecurity Analysis. Every CVE ID, CVSS score, quote, and patch detail traces to and matches a cited source; no fabrication detected. The only findings are two non-substantive automated warnings (allowlist + a 403 snapshot). No corrections record is warranted. APPROVE.
